. ractate ,, whose title has not come down to us, is an ency
clopedic compendium of basic nostic ideas, above all on protology and
eschatology terrestrial history is largely ecluded from discussion, and
the upper world and its development are not described in any detail.
artly academic in style (with numerous etiologies and etymologies). it
has the form of an apologetic essay intended for public dissemination.
he work was probably composed in leandria at the end of the third
century   ..   or beginning of the fourth.   t does not represent any known
nostic system, and draws upon a variety of sources and traditions. he
anonymous and otherwise unknown author refers to the subject of the
treatise in his   opening on this basis the work is now often referred to by a
modern, hypothetical title   n the rigin of   the orld (nrgld).   
 . . n many places, above all where the creation and destruc
tion of the world are treated,   nrgld   ehibits numerous parallels to
apocalyptic   literature,   especially   ewish   (e.g.,   thiopic   noch   and
 ubilees),   raising the question of whether it belongs to the genre of apoc
alypse. his possibility is ecluded by the lack of certain elements essen
tial to apocalyptic literature, for eample, narration of visions. periodiza
tion of history, the fundamental view of od as creator and lord of the
two aeons (realms), as also by the absence of esotericism, which is found
in many apocalypses. n certain ways   nrg/d   is more similar to ew
ish or hristian ibylline literature. bove all it seems to have been writ
ten with the purpose of disseminating nostic ideas, offering to the
interested public a defence of the nostic world view.   gainst the
assumption that   nrg/d   belongs to the literary genre of apocalypse is
the fact that the author presents his material soberly, without pathos, and
argues in a distanced and factual manner.   ith a view to the entirety of
the work, one could best designate it as a treatise or apologetic essay.

.     .   he date of composition can be only
approimately determined.   ertain ideas in the work appear to presup
pose anichaean theology. hus in its received form-as the deliberate
literary product of a single author (and quite apart from the historical
developm_ent of the traditions or materials that he   used)--nrgld
 probably was not composed before the time when anichaeism began to
have influence in gypt (end of the third century ..) and not later than
the early fourth century.   ny estimate of the date must take into account
the time necessary for reek transmission of the tet, its eventual transla
tion into optic, and further optic transmission before the mid-fourth
century copies found at ag ammadi.
he jutaposition of various kinds of ewish thought, anichaean ele
ments, hristian ideas, reek philosophical concepts, forms of reek or
ellenistic mythology, magical practices, and astrology, together with the
special prominence given to gyptian lore, points to leandria as the
place in which the original reek tet was composed.
.   .   he author of   nrgld   drew upon both nostic
and non-nostic works, according as their ideas were useful to his pur
pose.   ecause at least some of these works presupposed a quite special
system of their own or moved in a direction different from the author's
own thought, there sometimes are tensions, disharmonies, and contradic
tions in the tet. he relatively academic way in which he works is espe
cially evident in his citation, both direct and indirect, of other tets (now
lost),   and   in   cross-references,   summaries,   etymologies,   eplanatory
matter, and systematization, all of which present a striking contrast to the
narrative style that otherwise predominates in the work.   ut the author
not only adduces foreign material by way of completing or eplaining he
also can pass over in silence things that to him seem obvious. espite the
clear fact that he has utilized various prior sources-he himself refers to
seven or eight of them-in most cases it is not possible for the critic to
delimit or to reconstruct these sources with any conviction.
here is obviously a close connection between   nrgld   and the
 ypostasis of the rchons   ( , ),   reflected in many parallels (some
quite detailed) and by a notable similarity of style.   ut because of their
respective literary characters (the   ypostasis,   unlike   nrgld.   gives the
impression of an apocalypse), their different pictures of the universe, and
clear differences of detail,   a direct literary relationship seems to   be
ecluded. oth works could, in any case, be based upon some of the same
source   materia    this would   account for   the   connection   better   than
   dited in vo lume .
  .g., an apocalypse of orea. and possibly a nostic paraphrase of enesis as well.

assuming a relationship of direct dependence or of immediate successive
acts of composition.   nrld might have utilized the source or sources
in question more ehaustively than the   yposrasis,   or might have elab
orated upon them while maintaining the original style or else the   ypos
rasis   might contain a terse summary of the underlying material.   t is also
conceivable that the author of the   ypostasis   knew this material in a
somewhat different form (see also§ , below).
he author of nrgld thinks of himself as an educated apologist or
propagandist for his nostic world view. or this reason he tries to argue
in a factual and convincing way, and to support his opinion by reference
or allusion to other works. non-nostic ones among them, and thus to
lend it more weight. nrgld is directed to an interested, or potentially
interested, public at large. whom the author desires to persuade. hus it
is by no means a product of nostic esotericism.
 .    .   he work begins with a point of philo
sophical controversy concerning the primeval chaos, but quickly passes to
a detailed depiction of primeval events.   he complete absence of any
description of the upper world or any account of its development is strik
ing.   n contrast to what is found in many other nostic writings or sys
tems, all these matters are presupposed, occasionally being alluded to
without further ado.   n connection with the problem discussed in the
opening. the origin of the boundary (so-called "veil" or curtain) between
the upper and lower worlds is described, then the production of the demi
urge aldabaoth in a deliberate act of creation by istis ophia (aith
isdom), the main actor in the upper world.   henceforth aldabaoth
usually acts either alone. or else in concert with his "sons," the other
rulers (archons) created by him.   he creation of the world and of man
follows enesis, despite some discrepancies in sequence and detail.   ut
it also follows   concepts known especially from the pseudepigraphic
literature of udaism ewish influence and background is otherwise a
dominant and especially characteristic element in nrld, as in the
case of angelology, demonology, eschatology. and the etymologies that
are used.   he nostic interpretation of the given material varies consid
erably.   t ranges from a total revaluation. as with the arrogance of the
demiurge (using sa .  ) and the events of enesis , to the
relatively unaltered adoption of given ewish ideas and motifs, e.g.• in the
description of aradise (which, moreover. is created according to the
account of enesis , and not by the rulers).
he high point of primeval events is the creation of terrestrial man
this must be understood in the contet of the doctrine of primeval man
ehibited by this tet, a doctrine that is obscured by the presence of a
variety of motifs and concepts of differing origins.   ccording to it, after

the   arrogance   of   the   demiurge   aldabaoth,   his   correction,   and   his
renewed epression of arrogance, there appeared a heavenly primal man
(called   aggelos   angel. messenger] or "dam of ight"), not unlike the
"hird missary" of anichaeism or the nthropos (an) of the er
metic tractate   oimandres.    e unites with the consort of the demiurge,
who for her part corresponds to hysis (ature) in   oimandres.   erres
trial man is then made by the rulers, corresponding to en   and 
, "according to the image"   (kat' eikona)   of the rulers and "accord
ing to the likeness"   (kath' homoiosin)   of dam of ight.   n a counter
reaction, isdom (ife), who functions as redeemer in   nrgld   and
who also completes the rulers'   imperfect creation of man, creates a
"psychic" (animate) man.   he psychic man appears in various roles as
the dispenser of gnosis as the spiritual wife of dam, as the snake ("the
beast"), as a helpful instructor in aradise-yet all these beings are also
fundamentally   equatable   with   isdom   (ife)   herself.   he   original
lifespan of man was determined by fate (heimarmene), who is neither the
rulers' creature nor dependent upon them, and amounted to , years.
t the instigation of the rulers, however, this time span is reduced to 
years for dam, because he has disobeyed the commandment not to eat
from the tree of acquaintance (gnosis) while the life span of the posterity
of dam and ve, corresponding to the years taken away from dam, is
seventy.   he picture of events from enesis , with inclusion of en
 -, is clearly a nostic paraphrase of the iblical tet.
n contrast to primeval history which, after ve's violation by the
rulers and the birth of bel and her other children, ends with the epul
sion from aradise, terrestrial history is not an eplicit theme for the
author-even though events important to salvation take place in this
period.   n the beginning of cosmic history and at its end isdom
(ophia) functions as savior, either in the form of aith isdom (istis
ophia) or, in connection with dam and ve, as isdom ife (ophia
oe). fterwards in historical time the "blessed little innocent spirits"
(   -) collectively play the part of savior amongst mankind a simi
lar function is eercised by esus the ord (ogos), whose role as
revealer of the unknown    is basically unnecessary, and the "savior, .. a
figure whose identity and specific role are not made clear.

espite its long account of primeval history.   nrgld   is all in all
strongly oriented towards universal eschatology.   his is clear from its
repeated references to the last times and the long description of eschato
logical events, drawing upon many ideas, motifs, concepts, and technical
terms proper to apocalyptic literature.   o final day of judgement is
envisaged   those who are redeemed apparently already have become
saved before the end of the first aeon (age). hey partake of blessedness
according to two distinct grades along with the "perfect,"   nrgld
 also recognizes the "saved,'' who attain to a lower step of beatitude. he
essential nature of the final time, which is brought to pass at the instiga
tion of the upper world and above all by aith isdom (istis ophia), is
qualitatively superior to that of primeval time a repetition of primeval
events like those depicted in   nrgld   is impossible in the eschaton.
.   . .   he quantity of heterogenous material
used   by   the   author   makes   classification   of   nrgld   within   the
categories of the history of religions a difficult task. he work represents
no independent closed system of its own, and its author is obviously not
concerned with such a thing.   ut neither does it represent any of the
known nostic systems, even though its mythology offers numerous
parallels to the nosticism described by renaeus,   aer.   .,    and above
all to the ethian nostic system.   t is impossible to determine its
affiliations with more certainty, in the absence of information about the
structure of the upper world. n many places   nrgld   is reminiscent of
alentinian nosticism, especially in its tripartite anthropology, in the
integration of hristianity into a larger viewpoint, and in certain aspects
of soteriology. et it is not essentially alentinian, as is shown, e.g., by
the predominant or presupposed universal anthropological dichotomy. n
many passages anichaean influence is undeniable.   evertheless in
world view, mythology, doctrine of cosmic and human creation, anthro
pology, and eschatology   nrgld   is not basically anichaean.   he
author has simply used anichaean elements in fundamentally the same
way and to the same end as the other concepts, images, motifs. technical
terms, etc., that he has borrowed.

he world outlook underlying   nrgld   and the way in which it is
epressed show that the work is nostic so too, the recurrent nostic
motifs and the much used concept of "acquaintance" (gnosis), balanced
negatively by "lack of acquaintance" or "ignorance."   n anthropology,
the   principle   of   nostic   determinism   predominates   (cf.   especially
-).
mong the noteworthy peculiarities of   nrgld   are its recurrent
resumes,   numerical   combinations,   summaries.   and   systematizations.
specially curious is the introductory phrase "since (from) that day"
(reek retroversion   at'   ivrv nj, tiμipa) used before resumes or
before remarks that permit avoidance of a fuller description.   ince two
parallels between   nrgld   and the   ypostasis of the rchons   (see above
§) contain this phrase, it could be a characteristic of a common source
utilized in the two tractates.   owever, it also occurs ten times in
 nrgld   without any parallel in the   ypostasis,   a fact that is harder to
account for. he author is characterized by interest in numbers, either
alone or in combinations. he number seven or twelve in reference to the
rulers, or seventy-two in reference to the nations and their languages, is a
commonplace much more odd is the reference to the forty-nine demons
or the sity-four forms on the throne of the penitent ruler abaoth (who
deliberately takes up a mediating position, while himself partly showing
traits of the demiurge his father).   he author is especially interested in
the number three apart from a virtually unknown triad of archangels here
associated with abaoth, and the three-fold testimony of the animals of
gypt, there occur no less than four other trichotomies, which neither fol
low from the overall contet nor can be brought into harmony with one
another (first, second, and third dam three men or human classes three
phoenies three baptisms).   n contrast to the latter, a four-fold principle
of organization also appears (four races, consisting of the three kings of
the ogdoad eighth and highest heaven] and the race without king).   he
constant use of etymologies, especially for personal names, is striking.
he etymological eplanations, which in part can be verified, are a further
indication of the author's level of education.
n many respects   nrgld   is an important nostic work. n the one
hand, in this relatively lengthy tet we get a good insight into the thought,
method, and argumentation of a nostic author presenting to the public at
large his thoughts on the origin and end of the world and of man. n the
other hand, it shows the high level, freedom. and mastery with which such
a writer could utilize foreign, non-nostic materials. especially those of
very diverse character indeed some of the ewish views with which he
works are otherwise unknown to us. n this work we see a clear eample
of how an author's attitude toward the world and eistence has priority
over the concrete mythical form in which he epresses it.   nrgld   can

help us to understand why and how the nostic world view could assert
itself in dialogue with other spiritual movements and partly even replace
them.
. . he tet is relatively well transmitted in a few pas
sages, however, emendation appears to be unavoidable.   he seeming
corruption of quite a few optic passages-as also of other remarkable,
and often barely understandable, parts-may in fact indicate that the
received tet of the tractate has descended from what was only a provi
sional state in a multistage process of translation from the original reek
into optic.   n this perspective the tet that comes down to us would
represent an "opus imperfectum"    and editorial correction to a more
standard kind of optic would be a useless eercise. here restoration of
lacunas has been necessary, this could usually be done in a satisfactory
way, especially with the help of two parallels (a) a short fragment of an
identical tet (only the opening lines), preserved in the fragmentary 
 and (b) fragments of a ubachmimic optic version now conserved
in the ritish ibrary (olim ritish useum).   lthough the tet is not
attested in the original reek, this manifold optic transmission permits
the deduction that   nrgld   was a work that nostics considered to be
important and meaningful and therefore disseminated widely.   he main
tet printed below is that of   against it are quoted all variants from
    ll   they are found in the apparatus criticus. he ubachmimic tet
from the ritish ibrary is edited separately as an appendi, and a selec
tion of its readings is given in a special register of the apparatus to the
main tet.
he division of the tet into numbered paragraphs has been introduced
by the translators and has no tetual or graphic basis in the ancient
manuscripts.

. he   pository reatise on the ou/    (oul)   is an anonymous
treatise in the form of an ehortation to otherworldliness, using narrative,
paraenesis, and proof tets. t must have been composed in reek,
perhaps as early as   ..   . o trace of the original reek tet survives,
ecept for the present optic translation. he place of composition has
not been ascertained.
wo narrative episodes tell of the soul's fall from the presence of the
heavenly father into this life, where she is violated, degraded, and aban
doned.   he bears deformed children, weeps at her desolation, and prays
to the father. eeding her prayer the father restores her to her original
state, and from heaven sends her bridegroom and brother with whom she
is reunited in spiritual marriage. or this marriage she is reborn in youth
fulness, and in union with the heavenly bridegroom she ascends back to
the father.   iblical and omeric proof tets are adduced (attached to
catchwords), a chain of   ld and ew estament quotations characterizes
life in this world as prostitution, and the treatise cncludes with an ehor
tation to repentance.
. . .   oul   eemplifies no distinct literary type. t
treats topics which were discussed in philosophical treatises on the soul
starting in the second century   ..   (the nature of the soul, her incarnation,
her lot in this life, eschatology), and follows in part a typical latonic out
line found also in various ermetic and pagan nostic systems.   or a
clear statement of this outline cf. lotinus ... evertheless   oul   is
neither   philosophical   argument   nor sketch nor pure myth nor even
novella, despite its story of risk and disaster and a happy ending of re
united lovers. ts aim, which becomes apparent not only in the closing
ehortation but also in the paraenetic sections of the narrative, is ehorta
tion to otherworldliness.
.   . he work divides into a narrative, followed by an
ehortation, and then a recapitulation (in two parts) which focuses upon
the theme of repentance.   part from quotations of the bible and omer,
   lso known as   egesis on the oul.

the literary components easiest   to identify   are   narrative, ehortation
( to the end), and a self-contained eegetical composition on the
prostitution of the soul (-). here are also eegetical glosses
to the quotations   (-   -,   -),   hortatory comments
(-   -),   didactic   comparisons   and   definitions

tences in the hortatory conclusion   ("he beginning of salvation is repen
tance.... nd repentance takes place in distress and grief. ut the father
is good and loves humanity, and he hears the soul that calls upon him and
sends it the light of salvation") () various epressions which recur
throughout the composition sigh and repent, weep, mourn suffering,
affliction,   distress,   and   defilement   to   designate   the   soul's   bodily
eistence and () two concluding summaries, one patterned on omer's
elen, the other on the biblical odus from gypt, which also denigrate
earthly eistence and predicate deliverance on sighing and repenting. he
first part of the narrative aims to show that the reality of earthly eistence
is indeed suffering and defilement and that those who recognize it for

ciful and graciously heeds the penitent's prayer, the narrative itself-and
